config.systemPrompt:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose the smallest relevant tool set needed for the requested evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Verify only material uncertainty, and reuse evidence already collected instead of repeating equivalent investigation.
- When the available evidence is sufficient, stop investigating and provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

methodology: One read-only external-project analysis task executed through a DeepSeek LLM agent; it is operationally measured but not success-scored.
with_lynx: LLM has access only to the task-specific LYNX tool profile plus read_file.
without_lynx: LLM has access to read_file + grep only.
Fresh conversation per task x condition x run. No shared history or cache between conditions.
Paid microbenchmark protocol: exact deepseek-v4-flash, temperature 0, fixed per-round seeds, identical prompts/order/limits/runtime; no model or provider fallback.
Mode: live API calls. Suite: external-project.
Cost is estimated from token usage and configured pricing. Wall time includes API latency.
External-project budget: each condition is capped at 8 tool calls per task to prevent an unsupported workflow from consuming unbounded paid context.
ROI claims blocked when baseline invalid or sample size too small.

messages.content:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose the smallest relevant tool set needed for the requested evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Verify only material uncertainty, and reuse evidence already collected instead of repeating equivalent investigation.
- When the available evidence is sufficient, stop investigating and provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

I'm doing a lynx-fastapi cleanup sprint. Find symbols (functions, classes) in the codebase that have zero callers and zero usages — candidates for removal. List at least 5 concrete examples with their file paths and names. Do NOT invent symbols. Prefer a structural dead-code operation when available: its returned definition location and zero-incoming-edge evidence are sufficient; do not reopen every candidate unless that operation cannot provide the evidence, and stop once 5 well-supported candidates are established. Respond with JSON: {"unused_symbols":[{"name":"...","file":"...","kind":"function|class"}], "evidence":["..."], "summary":"..."}.

evidence: find_dead_code(kinds=[Function, Method, Class], path='fastapi/') returned exactly 5 candidates; total_returned=5, verification_complete=true.
Each candidate has zero incoming CALLS, USAGE, READS, and TESTS edges in the full project graph.
Each candidate has is_exported=0 (not re-exported as a public module-level symbol), confidence='high', and caveat=null.
The sibling methods APIRouter.get, APIRouter.delete, and APIRouter.options were NOT returned, confirming the graph discriminates correctly between referenced and unreferenced methods.

Source inspection confirms each method (lines 3648, 4030, 5166, 5548, 5930 in fastapi/routing.py) is a thin ~381-line wrapper that delegates to self.api_route(...) with a hardcoded HTTP method, duplicated nearly verbatim.
result.summary: Found 5 dead-code candidates, all on the APIRouter class in fastapi/routing.py: put, post, head, patch, and trace. Each is a ~381-line method that duplicates the same decorator pattern (docstring + self.api_route(…, methods=[…])) with no internal callers, usages, tests, or reads. The sibling methods get, delete, and options are actively referenced (excluded from results), confirming the signal is real. ⚠️ Caveat: These are public decorator methods of APIRouter that downstream users call externally. Removal would be a breaking API change; review whether they are documented/stable public API before deleting. If the goal is internal cleanup only, they are valid candidates for deprecation or consolidation to reduce the ~1,900 lines of nearly identical boilerplate.

errors: unused_symbols[0]: not a verified zero-reference definition: APIRouter.put|fastapi/routing.py
unused_symbols[1]: not a verified zero-reference definition: APIRouter.post|fastapi/routing.py
unused_symbols[2]: not a verified zero-reference definition: APIRouter.head|fastapi/routing.py
unused_symbols[3]: not a verified zero-reference definition: APIRouter.patch|fastapi/routing.py
unused_symbols[4]: not a verified zero-reference definition: APIRouter.trace|fastapi/routing.py

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.

I'm doing a lynx-fastapi cleanup sprint. Find symbols (functions, classes) in the codebase that have zero callers and zero usages — candidates for removal. List at least 5 concrete examples with their file paths and names. Do NOT invent symbols. Prefer a structural dead-code operation when available: its returned definition location and zero-incoming-edge evidence are sufficient; do not reopen every candidate unless that operation cannot provide the evidence, and stop once 5 well-supported candidates are established. Respond with JSON: {"unused_symbols":[{"name":"...","file":"...","kind":"function|class"}], "evidence":["..."], "summary":"..."}.

evidence: UJSONResponse and ORJSONResponse in fastapi/responses.py are explicitly deprecated; they are used only in tests and docs_src, not in any production code path within the fastapi/ package itself.

generate_encoders_by_class_tuples in fastapi/encoders.py is defined at line 115 and called once at line 126 in the same file; the result 'encoders_by_class_tuples' has no detected callers outside its own module.
_unwrapped_call in fastapi/dependencies/models.py is a private function with no detected references in the source code outside its own definition.
_impartial in fastapi/dependencies/models.py is a private function with no detected references outside its own definition.
result.summary: Found 5 symbols in the fastapi/ source package that appear to have zero callers or usages in production code. UJSONResponse and ORJSONResponse are deprecated response classes. generate_encoders_by_class_tuples is a one-use internal helper. _unwrapped_call and _impartial are private utility functions with no detected callers. Each is a candidate for cleanup removal, subject to a final manual verification of the 'impartial' and '_unwrapped_call' references across the entire codebase.

errors: unused_symbols[0]: not a verified zero-reference definition: UJSONResponse|fastapi/responses.py
unused_symbols[1]: not a verified zero-reference definition: ORJSONResponse|fastapi/responses.py
